nativeRange.habitat: Forest streams and slow-moving soft, weakly acidic to neutral water; associated with shaded, tannin-stained conditions with leaf litter and aquatic plant growth

decorPreferences: Dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Cryptocoryne, Anubias, Microsorum, Taxiphyllum)
Dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration
Driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins
Open mid-water swimming space for schooling

diet.notes: Micropredator in nature; generally accepts small high-quality dried flake or micro-pellet foods as a staple. Color and condition tend to improve with regular offerings of small live or frozen foods such as Daphnia, baby brine shrimp, bloodworm, and micro worms.

goodWith: Other small peaceful rasboras (Trigonostigma heteromorpha, T. espei)
avoidWith: Aggressive or boisterous species that may outcompete them at feeding

summary: Tiny, slender schooling fish from Borneo and Sumatra with a vivid orange-yellow lateral stripe edged in dark pigment. Generally considered a beginner-friendly community fish; best kept in groups of 8 or more in a soft-water planted tank.
careNotes: Trigonostigma hengeli is the smallest and slimmest of the three common Trigonostigma species — noticeably more slender-bodied than both the Harlequin Rasbora (T. heteromorpha) and the Lambchop Rasbora (T. espei). The lateral stripe is a warm orange-yellow with a darker border, rather than the solid black triangle of the Harlequin. Best kept in schools of at least 8–10; smaller groups tend to be shy and less active. A well-planted aquarium with dim lighting, a dark substrate, and some tannin input (driftwood, catappa leaves) is generally advised to bring out natural coloration and reduce stress. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water reflecting its Bornean and Sumatran forest-stream origins, though has been reported to adapt to neutral conditions. Cross-reference sibling species: [harlequin-rasbora] (T. heteromorpha — larger, deeper-bodied, broader black triangle mark) and [lambchop-rasbora] (T. espei — intermediate size, orange-red coloration, lambchop-shaped marking). Family taxonomy note: FishBase (2024) places this species in Danionidae; some older hobby sources list it under Cyprinidae. SeriouslyFish also lists Cyprinidae but FishBase is used as the authoritative taxonomy source here, consistent with sibling Trigonostigma entries.
breedingNotes: Generally considered moderately difficult to breed in captivity; some sources describe the difficulty as on the higher end of moderate. Spawning tends to occur most readily in very soft, acidic conditioning water (pH around 5.5–6.5, low hardness) at slightly elevated temperature. Eggs are deposited on the undersides of broad plant leaves in a characteristic behavior shared across the genus. Eggs typically hatch within 24–48 hours; fry become free-swimming within roughly one week and initially require very fine foods (infusoria, vinegar eels, or commercial fry food) before transitioning to baby brine shrimp.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for a species-specific page; SeriouslyFish is the first listed secondary for the fish-freshwater slice and was promoted to primary. Provided size (25–30 mm), temperature (23–28°C), pH (5.0–7.5), minimum tank (54L / 60×30 cm base), breeding notes, native range (Sumatra and Borneo), and habitat description.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Trigonostigma hengeli, Meinken 1956), family (Danionidae), order (Cypriniformes), native range (Sumatra and Borneo, Indonesia), max size 3.0 cm SL, temperature 23–28°C, hardness 5–12 dH. pH shown as 6.0 only (likely truncated data entry) — SeriouslyFish pH 5.0–7.5 used instead as it reflects the species' known soft-water range.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs deposited on undersides of broad plant leaves (shared genus behavior with T. heteromorpha and T. espei). Requires soft, acidic conditioning water. Eggs hatch 24–48 hours; fry free-swimming at ~1 week.
